data.title: My Two Top High-Yield Picks For April 2026: One Yields More Than 11%
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In this article, I would like to present two dividend-paying companies that are particularly attractive in the current market environment given their attractive Valuation, their ability to produce a stable cash flow, a strong position in their respective industry, their financial health, and their ability to offer investors both dividend income and dividend growth.

In 2026, ETFs with a focus on dividend income, such as the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) (with a positive performance of +13.16%), iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) (which has shown a Price Return of +9.28%), and the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM) (+7.55%), have clearly outperformed the S&P 500 (VOO) (+3.19%).

At the same time, these ETFs provide investors with significantly more dividend income potential when compared to investing in the S&P 500: SCHD currently offers investors a Dividend Yield [TTM] of 3.40%, HDV of 2.97%, and VYM of 2.26%, compared to the 1.09% of the S&P 500.

The main benefit for investors when opting for dividend-paying companies or ETFs is, in my opinion, that you get paid while you can wait for capital appreciation and that you do not need to sell any position of your carefully selected investment portfolio. It allows you to follow a buy-and-hold approach with no stress to try to time the market.

This is also one of the reasons for which I, personally, implement an investment approach with a focus on dividend income and dividend growth. The constant dividend enhancements of my portfolio allow me to become more and more independent from stock price fluctuations. I stay invested independently from the market's short-term price fluctuations.

In this article, I will write about my two top high-yield dividend-paying companies for this month of April 2026.

One of them is a Brazilian insurance company that presently pays a Dividend Yield [TTM] of 11.96%; the other one is a U.S. company from the Integrated Oil and Gas Industry, which offers investors a relatively attractive Valuation and modest dividend income combined with some dividend growth potential.

BB Seguridade Participações S.A.

For investors who are looking for a company that pays an attractive amount of dividend income, in combination with modest dividend growth potential, an attractive Valuation and strong profitability metrics, BB Seguridade Participações S.A. can be an excellent choice.

The Brazilian-based insurance company offers investors a Dividend Yield [TTM] of currently 11.96%, and a P/E GAAP [FWD] Ratio of 8.34, which stands 24.65% below the Sector Median.

The company's strong Profitability is underlined by its Return on Common Equity of 89.81%, which stands well above the Sector Median of 11.26%.

BB Seguridade Participações S.A. (BBSEY) currently represents 0.91% of The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio, and I have plans to include the company in my private portfolio as well. In both portfolios, I plan to set a maximum allocation limit of 2% to reduce the risk level of the portfolios.

The elevated risk level of BB Seguridade Participações S.A. is a result of currency risk, geopolitical risk factors, and a possible dividend reduction, which could have a significant impact on the company's stock price and on the overall return of your investment portfolio.

BB Seguridade Participações S.A.'s Dividend Payout Ratio currently stands at a high level (88.01%), which reinforces my belief to set an allocation limit of 2% for the company, allowing us to have a possible dividend reduction and a possible stock market decline that would have less impact on our portfolio's Total Return.

BB Seguridade Participações S.A.'s current Valuation

The table below shows BB Seguridade Participações S.A.'s current Valuation metrics. It can be highlighted that the company's P/E GAAP [TTM] Ratio of 8.36 stands 37.05% below the Sector Median, underlining that the company is currently undervalued.

BB Seguridade Participações S.A. according to the Seeking Alpha Quant Ranking

According to the Seeking Alpha Quant Ranking, BB Seguridade Participações S.A. is currently ranked 1st (out of 7) in the Multi-line Insurance Industry, 8th out of 681 in the Financials Sector, and 68th out of 4273 in the Overall Ranking, strengthening my investment thesis that BB Seguridade Participações S.A. can be an excellent choice for investors who want to diversify their portfolio globally while blending dividend income and dividend growth.

BB Seguridade Participações S.A. according to the Seeking Alpha Factor Grades

BB Seguridade Participações S.A.'s attractive Valuation, positive growth outlook, and excellent profitability grades are also underlined by the Seeking Alpha Factor Grades.

The Multi-line Insurance company receives an A+ rating for Profitability, an A rating for Valuation, a B+ for Growth, and a B- for Momentum, further strengthening my opinion to select BB Seguridade Participações S.A. among my two top high-yield picks for April 2026.

Chevron

Chevron (CVX) currently accounts for 2.71% of The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio and is among our four largest portfolio positions (behind Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A), Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), and Ares Capital (ARCC)). The company currently also represents 0.51% of my private investment portfolio, and I have plans to increase this proportion within the next few months.

Depending on your personal risk tolerance and investment objectives, you can overweight Chevron in a diversified dividend portfolio given the company's attractive risk-reward profile. However, I suggest setting an allocation limit of 5% to reduce company-specific concentration risk. Chevron currently pays a Dividend Yield [FWD] of 3.89% and offers modest potential for dividend growth.

Chevron, according to the Seeking Alpha Quant Ranking

Below you can find Chevron's current positioning according to the Seeking Alpha Quant Ranking. Chevron is ranked 6th out of 16 in the Integrated Oil and Gas Industry, ranked 6th out of 228 in the Energy Sector, and ranked 30th out of 4273 in the Overall Quant Ranking, reinforcing my investment thesis to select Chevron among my two top high-yield picks for April 2026.

Chevron According to the Seeking Alpha Dividend Grades

The chart below shows the current ratings according to the Seeking Alpha Dividend Grades. At this moment, Chevron receives an A+ rating for Dividend Consistency, a B- rating for Dividend Safety, a C+ rating for Dividend Yield, and a C- rating for Dividend Growth.

The rating confirms my investment thesis to see Chevron as an attractive core position for an investment portfolio with a focus on dividend income and when putting dividend growth and capital appreciation in second place.

Chevron Compared to Exxon Mobil and Shell

When comparing Chevron to other companies from the Integrated Oil and Gas Industry, it can be highlighted that Chevron's higher Dividend Yield [FWD] of 3.89% compared to Exxon Mobil's (XOM) 2.79% and Shell's (SHEL) 3.38%, as well as Chevron's longer track record of dividend enhancements.

While Chevron has shown 38 years of dividend growth, Exxon Mobil has raised its dividend 27 years in a row and Shell only 4 years, indicating Chevron as the superior pick for those investors searching to combine income and dividend growth at the same time.

However, in terms of Valuation, Shell is currently the most attractive pick in comparison to Chevron and Exxon Mobil: Shell's P/E [FWD] Ratio presently stands at 9.53, while Chevron's is at 15.23 and Exxon Mobil's at 13.53.

Conclusion

For this month of April, I see BB Seguridade Participações S.A. and Chevron as excellent investment options for those investors who prioritize dividend income while still focusing on dividend growth and capital appreciation.

With current Dividend Yields [TTM] of 11.96% and 3.77% as well as 5-Year Dividend Growth Rates [CAGR] of 28.72% and 6.01%, both BB Seguridade Participações S.A. and Chevron provide investors income and potential for dividend growth while offering an attractive Valuation.

I see BB Seguridade Participações S.A. as currently undervalued, which is underlined by the fact that the company's current P/E [FWD] Ratio of 8.34 stands 24.65% below the Sector Median, offering investors a margin of safety. Additionally, the company allows you to diversify your portfolio globally, which can help you optimize your portfolio's risk-reward profile.

While I suggest overweighting Chevron in a well-diversified dividend portfolio (limiting the exposure to a maximum allocation of 5% to reduce company-specific concentration risk) due to the company's attractive risk-reward profile, I suggest underweighting BB Seguridade Participações S.A. (not allocating more than 2% of the overall portfolio) due to the company's elevated risk -level, such as currency risk, a possible dividend reduction, and geopolitical risk-factors.

Including both BB Seguridade Participações S.A. and Chevron in your portfolio can help you balance income, dividend growth, and capital appreciation, effectively positioning your portfolio for strong risk-adjusted returns. At the same time, you can benefit from steadily increasing dividend enhancements.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DXC Technology: Favorable Read-Across From New Hires
data.description:
I'm still assigning a 'Buy' to DXC Technology Company (DXC).

The stock is cheap at a single-digit price-to-earnings ratio. A potential catalyst is better-than-anticipated future results driven by the recent talent infusion.

DXC's Q3 beat-and-raise was the focus of my prior February 2, 2026 write-up.

Bolstering The Executive Suite

Last Thursday, the firm published a media release revealing "leadership appointments and organizational updates."

"Consulting and Engineering Services" Or "CES" Management Team Additions

CES's 3QFY2026 operating income accounted for 55% of the company-wide figure. At the January briefing, the group shared that it's "investing in building AI-based offerings" to enhance "the performance of CES."

I infer from the above-mentioned that this segment's bench changes have meaningful implications for DXC's ongoing strategic pivot. My early-Feb analysis noted that it aims to "increase the contribution of AI-native, AI-enabled solutions" which are "less labor-intensive and better-yielding."

In my view, these moves could act as a catalyst for a recovery in fundamentals. As per S&P Capital IQ, analysts still see DXC recording revenue/bottom-line contraction in both FY26 (YE March) and FY27.

The CEO stressed at Morgan Stanley's (MS) "Technology, Media & Telecom Conference" in March that it currently "lacks the right product teams because there is a new DNA (needed) to develop agentic solutions." This talent gap issue should have been addressed by the latest hires.

Transition To More Profitable Assignments

AdvisoryX is described as CES's subunit that "combines strategic advisory with engineering delivery to help customers operationalize AI" in the April 16 disclosure.

At the MS event last month, DXC emphasized that its "Fast Track initiatives" (e.g. AdvisoryX) should boast "a higher gross margin or net margin profile." I believe they are on the correct path. In the past, it billed customers based on the hours spent. Going forward, the firm's pricing power will increase by aligning its fees with actual AI-driven improvements delivered.

Dan Albright, the new leader of this business, joins from NTT DATA. It's reassuring to know that the latter is Japan's "No.1 IT services market vendor." This sends me a clear message that DXC is serious about borrowing from the experiences of the leading players.

Boosting Customer Take-up

According to the mid-Apr release, "Srinivas Sai Nidadhavolu" or "SSN" has been tasked with optimizing the "go-to-market" approach for CES. I believe that his experience as "global lead for SAP at Wipro (WIT)" will play a big role in encouraging DXC's key accounts to embrace AI.

The company's CEO "Raul Fernandez" shared his own experience of spending slightly over a dollar to build an agentic product at MS's TMT conference. His opinion is that corporations aren't sufficiently capitalizing on the low "compute costs" for AI development.

Raul has attributed the lag in technology adoption to organizational inertia. He specifically mentioned that "procurement people are not exactly the fastest-moving groups."

In its LinkedIn post, SSN highlighted his role of collaborating "with our ecosystem partners to help clients modernize their enterprise platforms and embed AI." I think his ability to 'speak the same language' with WIT's installed base on prior modernization projects will be key to overcoming any potential resistance. That may lead to DXC realizing more deal wins and faster revenue recognition.

Establishing The Necessary Connections

Stan Clark previously created Cosmo Tech's "global partner ecosystem." In his new position, he will "architect DXC's next-generation AI partner model." This information is taken from the group's April press release.

An Overview Of Its Network

At the Q3FY26 call, management mentioned its 'Core Ignite' solution, which enables "banks to connect to fintechs" without "risky multiyear core replacement projects." They gave the example of working with "Ripple to integrate enterprise-grade blockchain digital asset custody."

The 'plug-and-play' nature of DXC's 'Core Ignite' is a big selling point for cautious financial institution clients. But DXC still needs to build and maintain ties with up-and-coming tech upstarts to stay relevant. That's why Stan Clark's appointment is crucial to prevent competitor poaching of collaborators.

Potential Of Unlocking Value Through Corporate Actions

The shares are currently trading at a depressed forward P/E of just 4.1x based on S&P Capital IQ.

DXC's CEO Raul stressed at the MS forum last month that "there's breakup value here that's above and beyond where we are today." He also touched on the fact that "we've had different suitors."

My thinking is that the firm is open to value-accretive asset monetization or even a privatization at a premium.

It has a " Global Infrastructure Services/GIS" segment whose profit margin was 4.4 ppts lower than CES in Q3. Given that CES is the focus for DXC now, I won't rule out a divestment of GIS. Its low-to-mid-single earnings multiple might also attract acquirers.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Bear-Case Scenarios

The new appointees may not have the right cultural fit with DXC. Premature departures will hurt investor confidence.

It's possible that DXC disposes of its businesses at a steep discount. A buyer could also make a lowball bid to buy out the company.

My Conclusion

I still like DXC as an investment candidate. The internal reorganization and value-unlocking potential are major positives.

I continue to compare it with "Kyndryl (KD)" as my Feb. 26 submission.

DXC's shares could go up by 66% (6.8/4.1), using KD's next twelve months P/E of 4.1x as a benchmark.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: My Top Pick Among BDCs
data.description:
Macro Headwinds In Private Credit

As I write these lines, the Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed for 53 consecutive days. It is impressive to see how calm global financial markets have remained so far, considering that global oil supplies have been curtailed by approximately 13 million barrels per day since the start of the conflict. Just a few hours ago, the German airline Lufthansa announced plans to cancel 20,000 short-haul flights over the summer in response to surging jet fuel prices. On April 20th, the credit rating agency Fitch also published a note mentioning that the Iran war and software disruption are the two biggest risks facing U.S. credit markets. As discussed in my previous article about FS KKR (FSK), those risks should also be front and center in the mind of anyone involved in private credit.

If we are indeed at the beginning of something much larger in private credit, FSK's elevated leverage and deteriorating asset quality could spell trouble. With a $13.0 billion investment portfolio at fair value as of the end of 2025, any meaningful amount of credit losses could impact its relatively small equity slice, currently valued at $3.1 billion.

Well before the start of the war on February 28, private credit markets were already under pressure and facing a growing wave of redemption requests. As highlighted in the table below, Blue Owl's (OWL) private credit funds have been at the epicentre of those capital outflows, with Blue Owl Technology Income Corporation or OTIC, facing withdrawal requests representing 40.7% of total assets under management. In turn, the world's largest private credit fund, the Blackstone Private Credit Fund or BCRED, faced redemption requests equivalent to 7.9% of assets under management. While Blue Owl opted to cap redemptions at 5.0% of AUM, Blackstone instead chose to provide liquidity to whoever wanted it.

This brings me to the Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), which could be viewed as a publicly listed equivalent to BCRED, despite a much smaller size. To be more specific, BCRED ended 2025 with $82.0 billion in assets, which is 5.6x more than the $14.7 billion in total assets reported by BXSL at the end of the same period. However, both investment vehicles are managed by the same team of investment professionals within Blackstone Credit & Insurance, one of Blackstone's (BX) core segments with assets under management of $443.0 billion at the end of December 2025. As a reminder, Blackstone is the largest alternative asset manager in the world with assets under management just below $1.3 trillion at the end of Q4-25.

Given its close association with Blackstone, the single largest player in private credit, BXSL necessarily has access to a strong deal pipeline and experienced investment professionals. I certainly think that this is a form of competitive advantage, especially compared to some of the smaller publicly listed BDCs out there.

Portfolio Overview

After researching Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), Ares Capital (ARCC) and FS KKR (FSK) over the last few weeks, the first element that caught my attention when looking at BXSL's portfolio was its almost exclusive focus on first-lien senior secured loans, which represented 97.6% of its total portfolio. This is in sharp contrast to FS KKR and its 57.8% exposure to first-lien senior secured loans. While BXSL and FSK are at two extremes of the spectrum, we find ARCC and OBDC in the middle, with exposures to first-lien senior secured loans of 61.0% and 73.0%, respectively. Given the challenging macro environment facing private credit, I view BXSL's quasi-exclusive focus on first-lien senior secured loans as highly attractive.

BXSL's unique portfolio composition is reflected in its 0.6% non-accrual rate, which is, to my knowledge, the smallest among publicly-listed BDCs. This is not surprising, as the 97.6% exposure to first-lien senior secured loans and the 0.6% non-accural rate are simply two sides of the same coin. Among the business development companies I have researched so far, FS KKR has the highest non-accrual rate, standing at 5.5% as of Q4-25. Once again, ARCC and OBDC are in the middle of the peer group, with non-accrual rates of 1.8% and 2.3%, respectively. Of note, all of the non-accrual rates mentionned above are based on cost rather than on fair value. In my view, it better reflects the real cost of non-performing loans, on top of removing the subjectivity associated with fair value estimates.

Software Exposure: A Case Study

Another element I would like to highlight regarding BXSL's portfolio is its relatively high exposure to the software industry, standing at 21% of the portfolio as of December 31, 2025. This compares against 11.1%, 16.4% and 24.0% for Blue Owl Capital, FS KKR and Ares Capital, respectively. At the end of the day, what truly matters to me is whether a loan is performing or not, and I think it is difficult to reach any hard conclusion looking only at a portfolio's industry exposure. For example, FS KKR has by far the largest non-accrual rate, despite having a relatively small exposure to the software industry.

Coincidentally, the topic of the day in the world of private credit is the software company Medallia. As reported today, the private-equity firm Thoma Bravo is preparing to hand control of the company to its creditor, with BXSL being one of the largest. As illustrated in the pie chart above, loans granted to Medallia represented 2.0% of BXSL's portfolio, although this is based on fair value. Looking at BXSL's 2025 10-K filing, we find that the BDC loaned a total of $396.0 million to Medallia at face value. This is equivalent to 6.3% of BXSL's net asset value reported at $6.2 billion at the end of 2025. Of note, entities managed by Apollo Global Management (APO) and KKR (KKR) are also lenders to the software firm, making Medallia one of the largest restructurings in the history of the private credit industry.

In fact, we only have to go back to July 2021 to find that Thoma Bravo acquired Medallia's equity for $6.4 billion. Now that the software company is about to be handed over to its creditors, most of the equity cushion is necessarily gone. It is also interesting to highlight that BXSL has marked down its Medallia loan to 77.7% of par, making the case that even first-lien senior secured loans on companies sponsored by leading private-equity firms can be impaired.

A Few Words On Valuation

The Blackstone Secured Lending Fund reported a net asset value of $26.92 per share at the end of 2025. Using the most recent closing price of $24.15, this is equivalent to a discount to net asset value of approximately 10%. Using the same methodology, we can also see that FSK, OBDC and ARCC are trading at discounts to NAV of 47%, 22%, and 4%, respectively. What I find interesting is that despite having a much higher non-accrual rate, Ares Capital is trading at a lower discount to net asset value.

As I discussed in a previous article, I view Ares Capital as one of the highest-quality BDCs on public markets. However, after researching BXSL, I have to admit that ARCC is now in second place. BXSL's non-accrual rate of 0.6% based on cost is truly unique in the BDC space, which is why I believe it does not deserve to trade at a discount much greater than ARCC's.

If Ares Capital is the largest BDC out there, I also think it is one of the best in terms of asset quality, hence why it is trading at a much narrower discount to NAV when compared to Blue Owl Capital Corporation, for example.

During my research on FS KKR, one of the main concerns I have raised is the fact that the company is seeking shareholders' approval to issue shares at well below the posted net asset value. Despite trading at one of the greatest discounts to NAV among all publicly-listed BDCs, FSK is also one of the few considering issuing shares. From this perspective, I view with a positive eye the fact that BXSL recently announced a $250 million share repurchase plan. If fully completed, the company could repurchase approximately 4.4% of all the shares outstanding using the current price of $24.15 per share. It remains to be seen if management will be active on the program or not, but in any case, I think it sends the right signal. After all, if the company and the management are not willing to buy shares themselves, why should I do so with my hard-earned capital?

As shown below, BXSL deployed $413 million in net new investments during the fourth quarter of 2025 alone. Interestingly enough, the weighted average yield on those new investments was 8.9%, a full point below the average yield on the investments that matured. To provide additional context, the Blackstone Secured Lending fund reported a weighted average yield on new investments of 12.0% for the full year of 2023, or much higher than the 8.9% reported in Q4-25. In my view, this is indicative of the increasing competitive pressure within the private credit industry. After all, hundreds of billions have poured into private credit over the last few years, making it one of the fastest-growing segments in alternative asset management.

From a capital allocation perspective, BXSL's management has the choice between making new investments at a yield of around 9.0% or buying back its own shares, currently trading at a 12.7% dividend yield. As a business development company, BXSL must distribute at least 90% of its taxable income to shareholders in the form of dividends. This obviously limits the amount of capital that can be retained to buy back shares, but some investments will come to maturity every quarter anyway. With a dividend coverage of 104% as of Q4-25, it is my personal opinion that it makes more sense to buy back shares on the open market than to originate new investments. If I were in management's shoes, I would make full use of the $250 million buyback authorization. But of course, that is easy to say from my yurt in the Canadian boreal forest. As mentioned by BXSL's chairman and co-CEO, Brad Marshall, in the Q4-25 conference call:

We have been trading at a premium for so long that trading at a discount is more of a recent issue. We have bought back shares in the past quite a bit actually. So we are not afraid to do so. We do have a lot of things that we need to manage, leverage levels, and making sure that we can support our existing portfolio companies. But I will say that given where the stock is trading, buying back shares is a very interesting price to do so.

Conclusion

With total assets of $14.7 billion at the end of 2025 and a non-accrual rate of only 0.6%, I view BXSL as the highest quality business development company among those I have covered so far, namely Blue Owl Capital Corporation, Ares Capital and FS KKR. Despite having a much higher non-accurate rate of 1.8%, ARCC is trading at a discount to net asset value of only ~4%, compared to a discount of ~10% for the Blackstone Secured Lending Fund. In my view, this is where the opportunity lies.

However, as expressed in previous articles about the private credit industry, I believe we are at the beginning of something much larger. Recent geopolitical events in the Middle East and the resulting surge in energy prices are only adding fuel to a fire that started well before the Iran war. Talking about the credit cycle, I think we have just rolled off the top and that there are still ways to go before reaching the bottom. What sign should we expect when we reach the bottom? From my perspective, the answer may have something to do with the Federal Reserve.

I will keep BXSL on my watchlist, as this is the BDC I want exposure to if private credit markets continue to deteriorate. My goal here is to stand ready if one of the highest-quality private credit portfolios becomes for sale. I may start to slowly accumulate shares at a price below $22.00, and would accelerate the pace below $20.00, and so on. If it never goes there, that's fine too, as I see plenty of other opportunities around.

In conclusion, I believe things in private credit will get worse before they get any better. However, my 'Buy' rating acknowledges BXSL's high-quality portfolio, which I view as even better than ARCC's.

data.title: AMD: Why I'm Going From Very Bearish To Confidently Bullish
data.description:
Performance assessment

My last view on Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has been terribly offside:

Elevator pitch

I am changing my mind on AMD due to the following:

  1. AMD securing large AI infrastructure commitments can lead to supercharged growth
  2. AMD's chips are making giant leaps in performance
  3. Agentic AI is leading to explosive demand for CPUs and enabling large price hikes
  4. There is still some valuation risk as AMD is still richly valued vs peers
  5. AMD has cleanly broken out of a key resistance level

AMD securing large AI infrastructure commitments can lead to supercharged growth

AMD is evolving from a mere chip seller to securing multi-billion dollar multi-year orders for massive data center buildouts. For example, AMD recently signed a deal with Meta (META) to deploy 6GW of its Instinct GPUs in Meta's AI data centers.

To help contextualize the scale here, an average American home consumes about 1.2kW of power in a day. 6GW is equal to 6 million kW, which makes it enough to power around 5 million homes in a day.

From a financials perspective, the CFO disclosed that we can expect "significant double-digit billions of dollars per GW" in data center AI revenue:

The Meta deployment is expected to generate data center AI revenue of significant double-digit billions of dollars per gigawatt. Revenue will begin in the second half of 2026 and ramp alongside our MI450 deployment with other customers.

- CFO Jean Hu in the Expanded AI Partnership with Meta and Multi-Year GPU Deployment Agreement Call

From this clue, I think a $15B/GW (a mid-teens rather than low-teens rate to account for the 'significant' adjective) is reasonable. Applying this to the 6GW, this implies an incremental data center AI revenue contribution of $90B. I expect this to be recognized over about 5 years as this aligns with the Feb 2031 expiry for AMD's issuance of a 160M shares performance warrant to Meta. So annualized, this is expected to result in an annual revenue contribution of $18B. This would correspond to a 108% growth on AMD's existing TTM Data Center revenue of $16.6B, painting a very rosy outlook indeed:

Note that Meta is not the only one signing large data center deals with AMD. In fact, this is leading to a wave of other contract wins with many more customers. For example, there's a 200MW deployment of AMD's Helios rack-scale systems in India and an estimated 10,000 Instinct GPU deployment in Korea. So clearly, AMD is seeing a flourishing amount of demand. The next sections goes into some reasons as to why this is happening:

AMD's chips are making giant leaps in performance

The MLPerf Inference 6.0 benchmarks is a standardized way of measuring AI chip makers' performance to assess how fast and efficient their hardware-software setup is. Based on these benchmarks, AMD's new MI355X GPU exceeded 1 million tokens per second (a measure of compute performance speed) for the first time, leading to a giant 3.1x generational performance leap over the MI325X GPUs. AMD also proved that its open-source ROCm software (competitor to Nvidia's (NVDA) closed-source CUDA system) can seamlessly link different generations of chips (eg. MI300X, MI325X, and MI355X) across different geographical locations (eg. US and Korea) to run global inference clusters efficiently.

Now, Nvidia still largely dominated in virtually every category in these performance benchmarks. However, AMD's results are providing a credible alternative, especially at a better price-point. Again, this is validated by the company's recent deal wins mentioned in the previous section.

Agentic AI is leading to explosive demand for CPUs and enabling large price hikes

While GPUs are used in AI model training, execution of autonomous tasks via AI agents relies heavily on CPUs. For this reason, hyperscalers are looking to ramp up CPU capacity:

hyperscalers are using agentic AI applications to drive high CPU utilization and thus looking to increase capacity “meaningfully.” As such, AMD said it feels good about growing its enterprise server CPU business and also mentioned that enterprise server CPU deals tend to come with incremental cloud business as well.

- Seeking Alpha news

A key product launch catalyst to watch out for this year is the Venice CPU, which is already seeing a high amount of interest among customers:

With Venice, like every one of our large customers want Venice the moment it comes out. And that kind of gives you a sense of how good it is because if you have power to spend, you want to spend your power on the best technology out there. And that's what Venice will be when it comes out.

- CEO Lisa Su in the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026

Now the high demand for CPUs is leading to supply constraints, which have also extended lead times materially:

Previously, the average lead time for a CPU was around one to two weeks, but now the wait time has prolonged to an average of eight to 12 weeks...

An executive with a server and PC distributor said that in some cases, the wait time for CPUs is now up to six months.

- Executives in the semiconductor industry, Seeking Alpha news

This is leading to price hikes of 10-15% and sometimes even more. I view this to be a meaningful gross margin tailwind for the stock:

On the whole, Wall St analysts have also been upgrading gross margins as a result of these developments:

There is still some valuation risk as AMD is still richly valued vs peers

AMD is trading at a 1-yr fwd PE of 45.3x. This is 38% higher than the 32.8x median of its semiconductor comps:

This 38% premium puts AMD in the overvalued side of the overall AMD valuation premium to comps chart, which has its median average at a 3.6% premium:

Another interesting thing to note, that makes me a bit more cautious about AMD's valuation is the discrepancy it has with Nvidia (NVDA), which as a 1-yr fwd PE of 24.3x. This is despite NVDA being the larger company that is expected to grow earnings at a faster clip than AMD in the near term (74.8% EPS growth for NVDA vs 60.7% EPS growth for AMD).

AMD has cleanly broken out of a key resistance level

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

AMD Technical Analysis

AMD stock has cleared a major resistance with a clean breakout and large bullish engulf candle. All of this is happening in the context of a broader uptrend. I see clear skies ahead for a continued run up.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish as AMD's chips are making large strides forward in performance capabilities, the company is securing massive orders for AI data center buildouts and there are gross margin tailwinds as CPU demand explodes and faces supply crunches.
  • Valuation: Bearish as AMD is valued richly vs its peers and vs Nvidia, despite a slower EPS growth outlook for this year.
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish as we have a clean breakout of a key monthly resistance level and there are clear skies ahead.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bullish, Strong Buy

data.title: GNT: Fundamentals For Gold Remain Strong, But This Fund Sacrifices Some Upside
data.description:
The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust (GNT) is a closed-end fund that aims to provide its investors with a high level of current income along with exposure to both natural resources and gold. This could be an appealing proposition right now, as the bullish case for many commodities, as well as gold, is very strong right now. Gold, in particular, is facing strong tailwinds from several central banks around the world actively attempting to increase their own exposure to the precious metal while reducing their exposure to the U.S. dollar. Indeed, as we will see in this article, central banks appear to be accelerating their diversification away from the U.S. dollar. Unfortunately, though, GNT sacrifices some of the upside potential from precious metals in exchange for income, so the fund could underperform gold during a strong bull market. The fund does have an 8.18% yield at the current level, however, which might be high enough for some income-focused investors to be willing to sacrifice a proportion of the potential capital appreciation.

About The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust

The website for the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust states that the fund’s primary objective is to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. From the website:

The Fund intends to generate income from short-term gains primarily through its strategy of writing covered call options on the equity securities in its portfolio. Because of its primary strategy, the Fund forgoes the opportunity to participate fully in the appreciation of the underlying equity security above the exercise price of the option. Under normal market conditions, the Fund will attempt to achieve its objectives by investing 80% of its assets in equity securities of companies principally engaged in natural resource and gold industries.

This description states that the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust primarily invests its assets in equity securities issued by natural resources companies and gold miners and streaming companies. A look at the fund’s largest positions reveals that the fund has a somewhat broad definition of what constitutes a natural resources company. Here are the largest positions in the fund as of April 6, 2026:

Newmont (NEM) and Northern Star Resources (NESRF) are both gold mining companies. These two companies are pretty much what we would expect in a fund that explicitly states that it invests in the gold industry. ExxonMobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) are energy companies that produce crude oil, natural gas, natural gas liquids, and chemical products that are made from these substances. Admittedly, some investors might not ordinarily consider energy companies to be in the natural resources sector. After all, a mining company is in the Basic Materials sector of the General Industry Classification System. Energy is its own GICS sector. However, hydrocarbons are a natural resource by definition, so ExxonMobil’s and Chevron’s presence here is not entirely inappropriate. Freeport-McMoran (FCX) and BHP Group (BHP) are both mining companies, so they are exactly what we would expect to find in a natural resources fund that invests in equities. Corteva (CTVA) and Nutrien (NTR) might be somewhat surprising to some investors. These two companies are generally considered to be fertilizer and agricultural chemical manufacturers. For example, Corteva manufactures fungicides, herbicides, seed products, various plant and seed oils, and products used to control plant diseases. Nutrien manufactures similar products to Corteva, along with fertilizers and similar products. While both companies consume natural resources in the manufacturing of their products, neither one is what many people might picture when they think about a natural resources company. However, both are suppliers to the agricultural industry, and the agricultural industry produces commodities. As such, it is not unusual to see companies such as these included in a natural resources fund. The same can be said about Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM), which processes agricultural products into compounds that are used in the manufacture of food products. Deere (DE) could arguably be considered an outlier here, as it does not engage in mining activities nor does it manufacture food or any other commodity. The company does, however, make equipment that is used in both industries. One could argue that Deere is a natural resources play because it acts as a supplier to the industry, and its sales do tend to pick up whenever mining activity increases. In that respect, Deere certainly has some natural resources exposure, even though it is not, strictly speaking, a producer of natural resources.

One thing that we notice about the fund’s top holdings is that it appears to have a reasonably balanced portfolio. After all, we see that its largest positions include two gold miners, two large mining companies that mine for various products, two companies that produce chemicals used in the production of agricultural products, a large agricultural products processing company, and a company that should be able to benefit from an increase in commodity production activity regardless of which commodity it actually is. A case could be made for a slight preference for agriculture-linked companies here, but overall, the fund’s portfolio looks pretty balanced. However, a look at the fund’s full portfolio tells a somewhat different story. The most recent annual report for the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust states that the fund’s equity portfolio had the following sector weightings as of December 31, 2025:

Sector

% of Net Assets

Agriculture

8.0%

Energy and Energy Services

22.0%

Food and Beverage

2.8%

Health Care

4.1%

Machinery

4.3%

Metals and Mining

56.5%

Specialty Chemicals

2.1%

The fund also had $1,124,370 worth of Allied Gold (AAUC) convertible bonds as of December 31, 2025. This company is in the “Metals and Mining” category shown in the table. If we were to add this into the table above, it would increase the fund’s exposure to Metals and Mining to 57.3% of the fund’s net assets as of December 31, 2025.

It might make sense to consider the fund’s convertible bonds to be part of its equity portfolio. This is despite the fact that convertible bonds are technically bonds and not equity. The reason for this is that these bonds can be converted into equities of the issuing company, and as such, convertible bonds frequently exhibit price movements that are more akin to common stocks than to bonds. For example, this chart shows the trailing ten-year performance of the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT), which tracks the Bloomberg U.S. Convertible Cash Pay Bond > $250MM Index, compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and the Bloomberg U.S. Convertible Bond Index (AGG):

Over the past ten years, investment-grade bonds have declined in price by 10.30%. However, both convertible bonds and common stocks are up more than 150% over the same period. It is very easy to see that the performance of convertible bonds more closely resembles common stocks than it does non-convertible traditional bonds. Due to this, we can consider GNT’s convertible bond holdings to be part of its equity exposure.

The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust only had 4.3% of its net assets invested in corporate bonds as of December 31, 2025. Thus, its exposure to this asset class is very limited compared to its equity exposure. This is certainly a good characteristic from the perspective of anyone who might be interested in this fund. Given the fund’s name, the investors who are looking at and considering this fund likely have a bullish stance on gold. There are a few primary reasons why someone might want to obtain gold exposure:

  1. They are momentum traders or swing traders who wish to profit from a continuation of the strong price appreciation that gold has delivered over the past few years;
  2. They are concerned about rising geopolitical instability and wish to have some exposure to gold as a safe haven trade;
  3. They are concerned about the fiscal trajectory of most developed countries and expect that central banks will end up monetizing national fiscal deficits;
  4. They believe that high inflation or even hyperinflation could be a near-to-medium-term possibility and want to protect their wealth.

Anyone in categories 3 and 4 probably does not want to have any bonds at all. A bond is simply a promise to pay a certain amount of fiat currency at some point in the future. In the event of inflation (or especially hyperinflation), a promise to pay some amount in the future is not worth very much. This is because the amount of currency received in the future will have far less purchasing power than it does today. In other words, bonds are unlikely to be a store of value in those situations. In fact, if inflation is high enough, investors in bonds might end up losing purchasing power and earning negative real returns. This actually was the case with U.S. Treasury securities over the past decade. An investor in the first two categories might be willing to own bonds, but it is just as easy to purchase individual bonds or bond funds as it would be to depend on a fund like GNT to give exposure to asset classes. As such, it makes no sense for GNT to have much bond exposure because that is not what its investors are likely to want.

The Covered Call-Writing Strategy And Underperformance Against Gold

As mentioned in the introduction, the strategy employed by the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold, & Income Trust is likely to underperform gold in a strong bull market. One reason for this is that the fund invests primarily in equities of gold miners, rather than gold itself. This chart shows the total return of the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) against the spot price of gold since the May 16, 2006, inception date of the gold mining fund:

As we can see, gold miners have substantially underperformed gold itself over the nearly twenty-year period. This remains true even if we assume that all of the dividends paid out by all of the gold mining companies over the period were reinvested. Admittedly, this has improved in recent years, and, in fact, over the past ten years, gold mining stocks actually slightly outperformed gold:

However, the long-term trend is still that gold mining stocks have underperformed the yellow metal. Thus, we can conclude that GNT’s preference for investing in gold mining companies instead of buying physical gold might be a headwind for the fund’s ability to keep up with gold price appreciation.

The second thing that could cause this fund to underperform physical gold is the simple fact that not all of its equity portfolio is invested in gold mining companies. The annual report states that the fund had positions in the following gold mining companies as of December 31, 2025:

The schedule of investments for the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust lists two different positions in Equinox Gold. Presumably, these positions consist of the U.S. (EQX) and Canadian (EQX:CA) listed shares, but the fund’s documentation does not state which position is which. In addition, we should note that some of these companies are more heavily focused on the production of silver rather than gold. The most important thing, though, is the fact that the fund has less than half of its assets invested in precious metals miners. This could weaken the fund’s ability to capture any upside produced by rising gold prices, but it could actually work to the fund’s benefit should other commodities outperform gold going forward.

However, the biggest reason why the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust could underperform gold during a strong bull market for the yellow metal is the fact that this fund employs a covered call-writing strategy. A covered call-writing strategy is an options strategy in which an investor (GNT, in this case) sells call options that entitle the buyer to purchase shares of common stock that it holds at a price that is determined at the time that the option is sold. In exchange for this privilege, the option buyer pays an upfront premium to the option seller, which the option seller is entitled to keep regardless of whether or not the option is ever exercised.

The best outcome for the fund with this strategy is for the call option to expire worthless. This allows the fund to keep both the upfront premium that was paid and the underlying stock. It can then write a new option against the same stock and collect another premium. This strategy works very well in a market in which the price of the common stocks that it holds is flat, declining, or increasing at a very low rate. In all three situations, the fund should be able to outperform the common stocks themselves. This is because the option premiums provide a return. However, it can cause problems in situations where the price of the common stocks is rising very rapidly. This comes from the fact that the fund is required to sell the stocks at the strike price of the option, regardless of the actual price of the stock. In a market where the price of the stocks is rising rapidly, the premiums that the fund receives may be lower than the capital gains that it would have been able to earn from merely holding the stocks. Thus, the fund’s covered call-writing strategy can cause the fund to underperform in such a market environment.

We can see this in the following chart:

This chart shows the total returns provided by the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust alongside those provided by the VanEck Gold Miners ETF over the past ten years. In both cases, I assumed that an investor used all of the distributions paid by both funds to purchase additional shares on the payment day. This may be an unreasonable assumption for an income-seeking investor who might want to spend the distributions instead, but overall, it provides a more accurate comparison of the investment profits provided by the two assets.

As we can see, GNT did a pretty good job of keeping up with the index fund from 2016 through early 2025. There were instances where the closed-end fund was beating the index fund, and it was doing so with less volatility (for example, the entire period prior to the COVID-19 pandemic). However, gold mining stocks began to deliver enormous gains beginning in early 2025. In fact, over the past year, the GDX index is up 90.60%:

The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust also delivered a very strong performance over the same period, but it did lag the index.

This fund offers a simple trade-off for investors. It will provide a higher level of income than can be obtained from index funds focusing on mining companies, agricultural stocks, or precious metals producers. However, it runs the risk of delivering lower returns than the index funds would in a strong bull market. It is up to each individual to determine if they are willing to make that trade.

A Bullish Case For Gold

Gold has been one of the best-performing assets over the past few years. Over the past five years, the price of gold has risen by 165.15%. That is a better performance than the S&P 500 Index delivered over the same period (72.62%). It is also, perhaps surprisingly, better than large-capitalization technology stocks (XLK), which have been the driving force behind the S&P 500 Index’s gains:

This level of performance may give some investors pause. I have seen numerous comments across financial social media that question whether or not gold will be able to hold onto its gains after such a strong run. Gold’s performance since the start of 2026 has definitely been weaker than it delivered in 2025, although it has still outperformed both the S&P 500 Index and large-capitalization technology stocks:

I will not dismiss the concerns about potential consolidation for gold in the near term, however. After such a strong run in 2025, it is possible that some owners of gold will be looking to sell and realize their gains. This could result in the price being rangebound between $4,500 per ounce and $5,000 per ounce for several months. Personally, though, I am not willing to sell the gold that I accumulated over time, as most of the factors that are providing tailwinds for gold are very much still present. I do not want to realize gains only to miss the next move up. Let us have a look at some of these factors.

One of the drivers of gold’s strong performance since 2022 has been central banks around the world buying gold. Central banks have actually been accumulating gold for quite some time, but it was not widely followed or reported in the media. For example, in 2018 and 2019, central banks purchased about 650 tons of gold (each year). In 2022, that figure was over 1,000 tons, and the 2023 figure was pretty similar. In 2024, it came in at a record 1,100 tons, and then fell slightly to approximately 800 tons in 2025. Thus, in every year since 2022, central bank buying of gold has been above the 2018 to 2019 baseline. Obviously, figures for 2026 are not available yet, but there are some other figures available that suggest that central banks around the world may be continuing to increase their exposure to gold.

Back in 1974, the United States made an arrangement with Saudi Arabia in which the Middle Eastern nation would require all of its oil customers to pay for their oil using U.S. dollars. In exchange, the United States would provide military protection to that nation. Shortly thereafter, Saudi Arabia would lead the other member nations of the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Nations to also require that their customers pay for imported oil in U.S. dollars. This forced oil-importing nations to accumulate U.S. dollars to purchase oil, and it resulted in the oil-exporting nations accumulating a sizable amount of U.S. dollars, which they recycled into U.S. Treasury securities. This was one of the major factors that resulted in the U.S. dollar dominating global trade and remaining the world’s reserve currency after the U.S. abandonment of the Bretton Woods system in 1971.

Unfortunately, it appears that the U.S. dollar’s status has begun to weaken. Back in 2016, the U.S. dollar accounted for right around 65% of the currency reserves of all central banks around the world. It was at 60.1% at the end of the first quarter of 2023, according to the International Monetary Fund. The International Monetary Fund states that the figure was at 56.8% at the end of 2025:

This is obviously a very sizable decline, and it happened very quickly. However, there does not seem to be any interest among central banks to increase their holdings of other fiat currencies. Between the third quarter of 2024 and the end of 2025, the U.S. dollar’s share of global central bank reserves fell from 57.93% to 56.77%. That is a 1.16% decline in roughly five quarters. However, most other major currencies were flat:

The euro appears to have benefited the most from this, but that was only a 1.09% increase over the period. The other developed market currencies experienced either flat to declining percentages of total central bank reserves, which at least partially offset the euro’s gain. Obviously, that money has to be going somewhere, and according to the International Monetary Fund, it is going into currencies that are “not separately identified:”

The International Monetary Fund does not provide publicly accessible data that tells us which currencies are seeing increasing exposure in global central bank reserves. What is clear, however, is that central banks around the world are reducing their exposure to the U.S. dollar, and they are not significantly increasing their exposure to the currency of any developed country or major trading market.

Further evidence that central banks around the world appear to be favoring gold over U.S. dollars can be found in the fact that central banks in aggregate hold more gold than U.S. Treasury securities. Back in October 2025, Crescat Capital reported that the combined total of all global central bank gold reserves exceeded the combined value of their U.S. Treasury holdings. This is the first time that this has happened since 1996. As Visual Capitalist states:

Seen as an alternative to heavily indebted fiat currencies, especially the U.S. dollar, the share of gold in central bank reserves has increased most among emerging market economies. China, Russia, and Turkey have been the largest official buyers over the past decade.

Visual Capitalist notes, correctly, that central bankers are becoming increasingly worried about the high debt loads and poor fiscal outlooks for many developed market economies. The International Monetary Fund issued warnings about this situation in its United States: 2026 Article IV Consultation back on April 2. Henry Paulson, former U.S. Treasury Secretary under President George W. Bush and former Chief Executive Officer of Goldman Sachs, expressed similar concerns. The International Monetary Fund noted in its report that the U.S. Congress needs to make “front-loaded fiscal adjustments,” which basically means that the international financing agency believes that if the United States does not cut its fiscal deficit immediately, then there could be a crisis in the U.S. Treasury market. It did not provide a timeline for the occurrence of such a crisis, however. It does appear to believe that the United States does still have time to prevent this situation, but it doubted that policymakers will be able to reach an agreement on how exactly to reduce the fiscal deficit. This appears to be the scenario that central bank policymakers are concerned about.

It is important to note that the International Monetary Fund was not only concerned about the fiscal outlook of the United States. It expressed concerns about nearly all developed countries, and it stated that these problems are structural, rather than cyclical. This means that it may be very difficult, if not impossible, for the problems to be resolved, as the resolutions would require making cuts to very popular government programs in nearly all developed countries.

Gold is generally regarded as the only asset that has no counterparty risk. A central bank or investor who holds their gold in their hands does not have to worry about a default. A sovereign bond, including a U.S. Treasury, can technically default if the issuing country chooses to do so. Alternatively, inflation might wipe out any nominal return provided by a sovereign bond (or any bond, for that matter). A company that issues a common stock can go out of business, causing its common stock to become worthless. Gold does not have any of these problems. If it is in your possession, it cannot default and it will always be worth something. This is likely to keep the demand for gold high as the world enters a period of high fiscal deficits and rising debt. This should be good for gold miners, as it ensures that the demand for their products will remain high. In addition, gold miners benefit from rising cash flows and rising profits whenever gold prices increase. As we have already seen, the share prices of gold miners do exhibit a correlation with the price of gold (even if they have underperformed gold over the past decade). The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust has just under half of its net assets invested in precious metals mining companies, so it should also benefit from the strong fundamentals for gold.

The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust As An Income Solution

As mentioned earlier in this article, the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust boasts an 8.18% yield at the current share price. This is substantially higher than the yield of nearly any broad-market equity index fund, as we can see in this chart:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index (SPY)

1.05%

NASDAQ-100 Index (QQQ)

0.44%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

0.92%

MSCI World Index (URTH)

1.43%

MSCI All-Countries World Index (ACWI)

1.48%

MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index (ACWX)

2.61%

MSCI Emerging Markets Index (EEM)

1.95%

The highest-yielding of these broad-market indices is the MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index, which is also the only index that tracks developed-market equities but specifically excludes the United States. As we can see, the three indices that track American equities (SPY, QQQ, and IWM) have considerably lower yields than the ones that include equities issued by companies around the world. This points to American equities being more richly valued and lower yielding than those from other nations. This has been the case for several years now, so it is unlikely to be surprising to anyone. The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust has roughly 25% of its total assets invested in companies that are based outside of North America:

Unfortunately, though, the fund does not provide figures for its exposure to American equities as opposed to Canadian, Mexican, or Caribbean ones. Canada, in particular, is one of the most popular countries for mining companies to domicile themselves in, so it is likely that the fund has more than a nominal exposure to that country. Indeed, as we have already seen, the fund’s portfolio does include several Canadian mining firms.

The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust also has a higher yield than either the global gold or global silver miners indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

MarketVector Global Gold Miners Index

0.68%

Solactice Global Silver Miners Total Return Index (SIL)

1.05%

These two indices (and the ETFs that track them) work fairly well as a way to gain exposure to those companies that produce precious metals. However, investors who want or need to generate a high level of current income from their assets are not likely to be satisfied with the relatively low yields of these indices. GNT’s substantially higher yield might be more appealing to those investors who still want exposure to precious metals mining companies along with some income.

There are very few peer closed-end funds that are comparable to the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust. Morningstar classifies it as a “Derivative Income” fund, which is the same category as other closed-end funds that write covered calls or use some sort of options-based strategy to earn income for its shareholders (such as Eaton Vance’s various equity funds that write index call options). However, nearly all funds in this category invest in a much broader universe of common stocks. For example, most of Eaton Vance’s option-income funds have a lot of technology sector exposure, but GNT has none. The various closed-end funds that invest in mining or natural resources companies do not usually write covered calls against them, which will result in them having a very different performance in the market. With that said, most investors who are specifically considering a position in GNT will likely also consider the mining equity funds as alternatives. With that in mind, here is how the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust compares with those funds in terms of yield:

Fund Name

Current Yield

GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust

8.18%

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited (ASA)

0.12%

Adams Natural Resources Fund (PEO)

7.97%

BlackRock Resources & Commodities Strategy Trust (BCX)

6.92%

As of the time of writing, GNT has the highest yield out of this group of peer funds. It is the only one of the group that actually writes covered calls against its holdings. Admittedly, the BlackRock Resources & Commodity Trust did write covered call options as part of its strategy for most of its history. However, that fund changed its investment strategy back in November 2025 and no longer writes call options. As such, ASA, PEO, and BCX are all financing their distributions through the capital appreciation of the stocks in their portfolios and the dividends that those stocks pay. They are not writing options, and thus investors in those funds may have more upside potential.

The uncapped upside potential of the peer funds is particularly noticeable with ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited, which has substantially outperformed all three of the other closed-end funds as well as the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR) over the past decade:

This chart assumes that all distributions and dividends have been reinvested, so ASA delivered this performance in spite of its low yield. None of the other three funds managed to beat the S&P 500, but BCX did manage to outperform GNT. Out of the three peer closed-end funds, the BlackRock Resources & Commodities Strategy Trust is the closest to GNT in terms of composition, but it does not have the capped upside due to the covered call strategy. It is somewhat disappointing that the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust underperformed that fund on a total return basis.

Recent Performance

The last time that we discussed the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust was on November 17, 2025. As such, approximately seven months have passed since we last looked at this fund. Most of the important events that have had an impact on our thesis for this fund have already been discussed, so let us just have a look at this performance.

The price of gold is up 17.58% since we last looked at this fund. However, the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust has significantly outperformed the yellow metal. This fund has seen its share price rise by 26.26% over the past seven months:

As we can clearly see, both assets substantially outperformed the S&P 500 Index. The outperformance of the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust compared to gold actually makes a certain amount of sense. The fund has exposure to the energy sector, which has benefited from the steep increase in crude oil prices ever since the start of the war in Iran. Gold itself has held up okay, but it did decline in March. This may have been due to the market’s fears that the Federal Reserve will not be able to reduce interest rates this year because of the inflationary pressures from the increase in the price of oil.

On a total return basis, GNT’s outperformance was even more pronounced over the period:

As we can see, over the approximately seven-month period, investors in the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust earned a total return of 30.73%. This was much better than either the S&P 500 Index or gold itself managed to deliver.

It is likely that most investors will be pleased with how this fund has performed over the past several months.

Distribution Analysis

The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.06 per share ($0.72 per share annually). This gives the fund an 8.18% yield at the current share price.

As is the case with some other funds that invest in commodity producers, the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust has been steadily increasing its distribution over the past few years. Back in September of last year, the fund was only paying a distribution of $0.04 per share monthly:

As I have mentioned in past articles, it is not usual for a closed-end fund to see its share price increase whenever it raises its distribution. It is, therefore, possible that the distribution growth has been a contributing factor to GNT’s strong recent performance. As is always the case, however, we should have a look at the fund’s finances in order to determine how well it is actually covering the distributions that it pays out.

As of the time of writing, the newest financial document that is available for the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust is the annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025. This is a newer report than the one that we had available the last time that we looked at this fund, so let us review it.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, GNT reported a net investment income of $2,092,338. This was not sufficient to cover the $8,259,773 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period.

Fortunately, the fund did manage to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, GNT reported net realized gains of $9,833,972 along with net unrealized gains of $34,923,403. Overall, the fund’s portfolio produced $46,849,713 in investment profits over the period:

This was more than sufficient to cover the distributions that this fund paid out over the same period. While that is nice, regular readers are likely aware that it is preferable whenever a fund can completely cover all of its distributions solely with net investment income and net realized gains. This is simply because unrealized gains are not necessarily permanent and can be erased by weakness in the market. For the twelve-month period, this fund’s net investment income plus net realized gains was $11,926,310. It paid out $1,691,540 of that to its preferred stockholders. This left it with $10,234,770 available to pay the distributions to the common stockholders. It paid out less than that, so we can see that this fund did manage to fully cover all of the distributions solely with its realized investment profits. This is exactly what we would normally like to see.

Valuation

Shares of the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust are currently trading at a 0.91% premium on net asset value. This is a worse price than the 3.91% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

The fund’s shares are also looking rather expensive compared to their long-term average. Over the past five years, shares of GNT have traded at prices ranging from a 19.84% discount to a 2.01% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 12.28% discount on net asset value. Clearly, the five-year average price is a lot better than the fund’s price today.

Here is how the fund’s current price compares to that of its peer CEFs:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust

0.91%

ASA Gold and Precious Metals Limited

(13.98%)

Adams Natural Resources Fund

1.00%

BlackRock Resources & Commodities Strategy Trust

0.75%

With the exception of ASA, none of these funds looks very attractively priced right now. After all, three of them are trading at levels that are higher than the price of their underlying assets. In the case of GNT, the fund is largely in line with its peers, but considering that it is quite expensive compared to its long-term average, it could be best to wait until the price comes down before acquiring or increasing a position in the fund.

Expenses

The most recent annual report for the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust states that the fund has an expense ratio of 1.95%.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust is an interesting closed-end fund that holds a portfolio of equities issued by commodity and precious metals producers and then writes call options against these equities in order to earn income. The strategy does pretty well from an income perspective, as the fund’s fully-covered 8.18% yield makes clear. In addition, the investment case for precious metals remains very strong as central banks and investors all over the world appear to be getting increasingly worried about the high levels of debt and high fiscal deficits of the United States and other developed countries. Unfortunately, though, this fund’s strategy does prevent it from fully benefiting from the capital appreciation of the common stocks that it holds in its portfolio. Investors who simply want a high level of income might find the trade-off worthwhile, though.

data.title: April ECB Cheat Sheet: Ready, Aim, Hold
data.description:
By Francesco Pesole | Michiel Tukker | Carsten Brzeski

The European Central Bank will likely keep rates on hold at the 30 April meeting. That’s been hinted at by several Governing Council members lately, who have stressed the lack of new information and the absence of urgency to act. In that sense, April will mainly serve as a reality check for rate expectations, with markets currently pricing around 50bp of tightening by year‑end.

In the table below, we outline four scenarios from the most dovish to the most hawkish (with the latter including a surprise hike) and what they would mean for rates and the euro. In our baseline, we expect the ECB to stick to a cautious tone while emphasising that both inflation and growth risks remain material. President Christine Lagarde may not give too much away in the press conference, but we expect she’ll give enough implicit hints that a summer rate hike is a reasonable expectation.

Four scenarios for the ECB's 30 April meeting

Rates expect the ECB to act when needed

With just 10% priced in, markets are not expecting the ECB to hike this meeting, but the stakes are higher for June. Over the past weeks, markets have roughly priced in between 20bp and 40bp of hikes by June. Markets are still speculating about the ECB’s reaction function, and any hints about a June move will be taken on board.

So far, long-term inflation swaps have remained very stable, reflecting the confidence in the ECB’s inflation targeting. Despite large swings in 2Y inflation swaps, the 5Y5Y inflation forward only bumped up from around 2.08% in February to 2.14%, a minor blip by historical standards. To put this into context, the 5Y5Y rose above 2.6% in 2023. To keep market inflation expectations well-anchored, a hike might be needed eventually if oil stays high or rises further.

The big surprise would be the ECB over-delivering compared to market expectations by hiking already at this meeting. The pass-through to longer-dated rates could, however, be limited in this tail risk scenario. If the move were to be packaged in a dovish way, markets would likely interpret the hikes as a front-loading of future tightening and not a radical change in reaction function. In this case, we would see a mild bear-flattening of the curve.

If, on the other hand, the hike is complemented with a hawkish narrative, we do see the risk of a perceived policy error. The ECB could be seen as pressing the brakes too hard, which could damage market sentiment, weighing on longer-dated rates.

In a contrasting scenario where the ECB turns overly dovish, dismissing inflation risks as transitory (without actually using the word “transitory”), we might see a significant steepening of the curve. In this case, 2Y rates might edge lower on fewer rate hike expectations, but the increased long-term inflation expectations would push up rates from 5Y and beyond. Again, not our base case, but still a risk to consider.

FX: Surprise needed, or rates stay secondary for EUR/USD

The notion that the ECB is responding more hawkishly than the Federal Reserve to the ongoing energy shock underpinned EUR/USD resilience initially and then helped fuel the de‑escalation rally. The two‑year EUR/USD swap rate differential is now around 20bp tighter than pre‑war levels, and its mostly positive correlation with oil prices suggests markets see EUR front‑end rates as more responsive to fresh escalation risks.

That said, while the front‑end rates beta to EUR/USD has recovered after briefly dropping close to zero in March, global equities are currently a much bigger driver for the pair. Rates, equities and oil are clearly intertwined, but recent sessions have shown that EUR/USD’s ability to hold above 1.170 largely depends on risk sentiment staying resilient, even when oil prices face upward pressure.

In other words, it would take a real surprise from the ECB for the impact on EUR/USD to be both material and lasting. Absent that, central bank policy divergence is likely to take a back seat again to oil moves and, above all, global equity performance.

data.title: How To Do Investment Research (2026 Updated Version)
data.description:
To provide an orientation to those beginning their education and to offer a brush-up for veterans, we've compiled a guide on best practices for the investment research process. This is an updated version of an article Seeking Alpha presented to analysts and readers on how to conduct investment research. Please share your ideas below in the comments section.

Put Your Investment Research in Context

The universal right way to invest may not exist - everyone has different preferences for risk, cognitive strengths and weaknesses, future cash needs, and time horizons. But you can convince readers that you have useful insight (and confront some of your own imperfections with honesty) by being transparent about your own circumstances and explaining those circumstances to your audience.

Remember, whether you're discussing your own portfolio decisions or analyzing a particular company, context is the lifeblood of investment research. Make sure you're situating the evidence you've unearthed so that someone who's new to the story can incorporate the discussion into his or her own decision process.

How to Do Investment Research

We believe that sound research forms the basis from which all success at Seeking Alpha follows.

If you're writing about an individual company, start by reading the most recent 10-K filing. This annual report gives highly detailed insight into company business models, recent performance, and key risks.

Don't stop with the 10-K. Use Seeking Alpha's SEC filing database to get more key primary source information. Make sure you know the benefits of going directly to the filings vs. relying on third-party data sources.

Armed with many of the details regulators have decided companies need to share with investors, it can help to contrast what you know with what the company management says in its press releases. If the narrative the company is feeding to the press differs from what it's disclosing to the SEC, there's usually a big opportunity to inform investors - the basis of a successful Seeking Alpha article.

How to Write Investment Analysis

Investment analysts need to know the difference between factual information/data and their reasoned opinions and inferences. Successful Seeking Alpha articles provide a balance of fact and opinion. Avoid rants and screeds that are heavy on opinion and light on fact; conversely, avoid recaps and overviews that gather readily available information without injecting your own independent perspective.

Analysts have a variety of approaches, but it's important to be comprehensive along two dimensions: Qualitative and quantitative information. Make sure you have a solid understanding of the fund or company's strategy and competitive position (qualitative) as well as how that strategy plays out in the financial statements (quantitative). When the connection between the qualitative and quantitative is strong, you have the beginnings of a very compelling investment analysis.

How to Credit Sources in Investment Analysis

In investment research, attention to detail is key. And nowhere is this more true than in the basic but absolutely critical matter of giving proper credit to one's sources. Know when to use direct quotes, paraphrases, and summaries when presenting material from outside sources.

We ask that analysts first use resources available at Seeking Alpha (news, filings, press releases, Quant ratings and charts, etc.) when adding in source material.

How to Value a Stock

The investment community abounds with variations on the cliché that valuation models are never right, but they're often useful. To land closer to the "useful" side of things, it can help to understand how different models work and when/why to use one compared to another.

Get familiar with the classic discounted cash flow model. Then, as you're deploying it, make sure you're putting discounted cash flows in proper context. Understand that cash flow estimates can be refined and improved and that caveats apply.

Some analysts prefer to use EBITDA as a basis for financial decisions. But be careful using EBITDA - it has some known flaws to go with its benefits. Check the inputs to your model - are you using traditional and often more conservative GAAP accounting, or are you making some adjustments?

Maybe you're just going with a handy P/E ratio. If so, make sure you understand how the P/E ratio works.
